chunk_summary: Stepwise escalation for persistent feeding problems in 6–24 months involves staged interventions to address texture progression, energy density, and responsive feeding, with structured follow-up and therapy for severe cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of texture progression in complementary feeding to prevent feeding delays.
Learn the staged escalation plan for managing persistent feeding problems in children aged 6–24 months.
Identify red flags for aspiration and criteria for medical evaluation or inpatient referral.
Apply dietary fortification strategies for faltering growth in young children.
key_facts: Stage 1: Immediate corrections include planned meals/snacks, energy-dense foods, and responsive feeding within 30-minute meals.
Stage 2: Follow-up in ~2 weeks includes 24-hour bowl recall, weight monitoring, and advancing texture or diversity goals.
Stage 3: Escalate to therapy or medical evaluation if no progress within 2–4 weeks or if aspiration red flags exist (e.g., cough, choke, wet voice).
Aspiration risk may require inpatient referral for respiratory compromise or complicated wasting.
Guidance for faltering growth supports dietitian referral and fortification when energy/nutrient density cannot be achieved by caregivers alone.
reference_quote: Guidance for faltering growth supports dietary fortification and dietitian referral when energy and nutrient density must increase beyond what caregivers can achieve alone. (NICE NG75)
